content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
December 2,
1992
A.
Brock Edmunds
Public Affairs Paralegal
Liberty Mutual
175 Berkeley Street
Boston, Massachusetts
02117-0140
Re:
Your Request for Confirmation
of Telephone Advice
Our File No.
1-92—602
Dear Ms.
Ethuunds:
You have requested confirmation of telephone advice provided
to you concerning the campaign provisions of the Political Reform
Act
(the “Act”).l/
Because your letter does not identify a
particular person or concern a specific pending governmental
decision but rather seeks general guidance,
we are treating your
request as one for informal assistance.2/
Your letter of October 20,
1992, correctly summarizes my
advice that to qualify as a “committe&’ pursuant to Section
82013(a),
a “person” must receive $1,000 or more during a calendar
year for “political purposes.”
Section 82013(a)
defines “committee” in relevant part as:
Any person or combination of persons who directly or
indirectly receives contributions totaling $1,000
or more in a calendar year...
“Person” means an individual, proprietorship,
firm,
partnership, joint venture,
syndicate, business trust,
company
corporation, association,
committee,
and any other organization or
group of persons acting in concert.
(Section 82047.)
1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000,
g.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2/
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660


A.
Brock Edmunds
Our File No.
1—92—602
Page
2
A person who receives money for political purposes has
received “contributions,” and must register and file disclosure
reports as a recipient committee under Section 82013 (a).
The term “contribution” is defined in relevant part as:
Any monetary or nonmonetary payment made for political
purposes for which full and adequate consideration is
not made to the donor.
(Regulation 18215.)
Regulation 18215 defines “a payment for political purposes,”
in relevant part,
as a payment:
(1)
For the purpose of influencing or attempting
to influence the action of the voters for or
against the nomination or election of a candidate or
candidates,
or the qualification or passage of any
measure...
In summary, in order to incur a filing obligation as a
recipient committee under the Act,
a person must receive $1,000 or.
more in a calendar year for political purposes.
If less thati
$1,000 is received during the calendar year, there is no reporting
requirement under the Act.
If you have any questions, please give me a call at 916/322-
5662.
Sincerely,
rrL
Mary
n Kvasager
Political Reform Consultant

sections.conclusion: In summary, in order to incur a filing obligation as a recipient committee under the Act, a person must receive $1,000 or more in a calendar year for political purposes. If less than $1,000 is received during the calendar year, there is no reporting requirement under the Act.
sections.question_synthetic: What threshold must be met for a person or entity to qualify as a 'committee' under the Political Reform Act and trigger campaign finance reporting requirements?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: A person or entity qualifies as a 'committee' subject to registration and disclosure requirements only if it receives $1,000 or more in a calendar year for political purposes; amounts below this threshold do not trigger reporting obligations.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an informal advice letter confirming previously provided telephone advice. No formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION sections are present; both question and conclusion were extracted from the body text. The letter explicitly states it provides informal assistance without immunity protections.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What threshold must be met for a person or entity to qualify as a 'committee' under the Political Reform Act and trigger campaign finance reporting requirements?

CONCLUSION: In summary, in order to incur a filing obligation as a recipient committee under the Act, a person must receive $1,000 or more in a calendar year for political purposes. If less than $1,000 is received during the calendar year, there is no reporting requirement under the Act.

(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).) 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660 A. Brock Edmunds Our File No. 1—92—602 Page 2 A person who receives money for political purposes has received “contributions,” and must register and file disclosure reports as a recipient committee under Section 82013 (a). The term “contribution” is defined in relevant part as: Any monetary or nonmonetary payment made for political purposes for which full and adequate consideration is not made to the donor. (Regulation 18215.) Regulation 18215 defines “a payment for political purposes,” in relevant part, as a payment: (1) For the purpose of influencing or attempting to influence the action of the voters for or against the nomination or election of a candidate or candidates, or the qualification or passage of any measure... In summary, in order to incur a filing obligation as a recipient committee under the Act, a person must receive $1,000 or. more in a calendar year for political purposes. If less thati $1,000 is received during the calendar year, there is no reporting requirement under the Act. If you have any questions, please give me a call at 916/322- 5662. Sincerely, rrL Mary n Kvasager Political Reform Consultant
embedding.summary: The FPPC confirms informal telephone advice regarding the definition of 'committee' under the Political Reform Act, clarifying that the $1,000 annual threshold for contributions designated for political purposes determines whether an entity must register and file as a recipient committee.
